neighborhoods.name: Brownsville
primary_groups: Mexican and Mexican American families (majority population)
neighborhoods.character: The southernmost city in Texas, sitting directly across the Rio Grande from Matamoros, Mexico. Brownsville was Mexican long before it was American — the culture, language, and family networks crossed the river without regard for the border drawn in 1848. The city was divided between Anglo commercial interests that controlled the land and the Mexican American majority that worked it. Fort Brown, the military post that gave the city its name, was a constant federal presence. The old town center mixed Mexican and Anglo architecture, while the Mexican barrios spread south and west toward the river.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: Brownsville was Mexican before it was Texan — if {ancestor_name} came to the valley through Brownsville, they crossed a river that divided two nations but not one community, and the family networks that sustained them stretched across the Rio Grande as naturally as the water flowed.

neighborhoods.name: McAllen / Edinburg
neighborhoods.character: McAllen and Edinburg sat at the center of the valley's agricultural boom — the area exploded in population after 1910 when irrigation projects turned brushland into citrus groves and vegetable farms. Anglo developers from the Midwest promoted the valley as a tropical paradise and bought up Mexican-owned ranchlands at distressed prices. The towns were rigidly segregated — Anglo north side, Mexican south side. Edinburg was the Hidalgo County seat, where land records documented the transfer of Mexican-grant lands to Anglo ownership.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: McAllen was the center of the citrus boom that transformed the valley — but the transformation ran on Mexican labor. If {ancestor_name} worked the groves around McAllen, they picked grapefruit that was shipped north with a 'Texas Magic Valley' label while the pickers' own neighborhood had no paved streets.

neighborhoods.name: Harlingen / San Benito / Weslaco
neighborhoods.character: The mid-valley towns along US Highway 83 — the lifeline of the Rio Grande Valley. These were agricultural service towns surrounded by cotton fields and vegetable farms. Each had its Anglo downtown and its Mexican colonia. San Benito was particularly known for its cotton compress and the labor camps that surrounded it. Weslaco (named for the W.E. Stewart Land Company that developed it) was a planned Anglo agricultural town with Mexican labor quarters on the periphery. The pattern was uniform — Anglo prosperity visible on the highway, Mexican poverty hidden a few blocks away.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: The mid-valley towns were interchangeable in one way — every one had an Anglo main street and a Mexican colonia where {ancestor_name}'s family would have lived, separated by a few blocks and an economic chasm that the citrus labels and Chamber of Commerce brochures never mentioned.

neighborhoods.name: Mercedes / Mission / La Feria
neighborhoods.character: Smaller agricultural towns in the western valley, closer to the river. Mercedes and Mission were citrus centers — grapefruit orchards stretched in every direction. La Feria was cotton and vegetable country. These towns were more intimate than McAllen or Brownsville — everyone knew everyone in the Mexican community, and the patron-laborer relationships between Anglo growers and Mexican workers had a feudal quality. Some grower families maintained the same worker families across generations, creating bonds of dependency that looked like loyalty from above and survival from below.
key_institutions: Valley irrigation canals and pump stations
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: In the small valley towns, the grower knew {ancestor_name} by name — the relationship looked like loyalty from the Anglo side and survival from the Mexican side, and the family worked the same orchards year after year because the alternative was the road to somewhere else with even fewer guarantees.

neighborhoods.name: Colonias (Unincorporated Settlements)
neighborhoods.character: The colonias were — and remain — unincorporated settlements on the outskirts of valley towns, often without running water, sewers, or paved roads. Developers sold cheap lots to farm worker families on installment plans, but the lots lacked basic infrastructure because they were outside city limits and therefore outside city services. By the 1960s, hundreds of colonias housed tens of thousands of people in conditions that shocked observers who visited them. The colonias were the physical manifestation of the valley's economic hierarchy — Anglo towns with services surrounded by Mexican communities without them.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: The colonias were where the valley's promises ended — if {ancestor_name}'s family bought a lot in an unincorporated settlement outside McAllen or Brownsville, they got a deed but no running water, a street address but no paved road, and the knowledge that the city whose crops they harvested stopped at the line where their neighborhood began.

major_employers.name: Citrus Groves (Grapefruit and Orange)
major_employers.wages: Piece rates by the box — pickers earned $0.05-0.15 per box (1940s-1950s), with skilled workers filling 60-100 boxes per day for $3-8/day. Packing shed workers (often women) earned $0.40-0.65/hr. Bracero workers' wages were set by contract but frequently reduced by deductions. The valley's grapefruit industry was the economic engine — Texas Ruby Red grapefruit commanded premium prices.
major_employers.working_conditions: Citrus work was year-round but peaked during the winter harvest (November-April). Workers climbed ladders carrying heavy canvas bags, cutting fruit by hand. Thorns tore skin and clothing. The heat was less extreme than California's Central Valley but combined with high humidity. Pesticide exposure was chronic — aerial spraying continued while workers were in the groves. The citrus industry was controlled by Anglo growers' associations that coordinated labor supply and wages.
major_employers.narrative_hook: The Ruby Red grapefruit made the valley famous — if {ancestor_name} picked citrus, they climbed ladders in humid heat, filled bag after bag, and watched the fruit they harvested get packed under labels that sold a tropical paradise while the pickers lived in colonias without plumbing.

major_employers.name: Cotton Farming
major_employers.wages: Piece rates by the hundredweight — $0.75-1.50 per 100 pounds (1940s-1950s). Valley cotton was a supplement to the citrus economy, grown in the same fields between citrus seasons. The cotton harvest drew families from Mexico and from deeper in Texas. Valley cotton workers were also recruited by labor contractors to travel north to the cotton fields of West Texas, the sugar beet fields of Michigan and Minnesota, and the vegetable farms of the Midwest.
major_employers.working_conditions: Cotton in the valley was smaller-scale than in West Texas or the Mississippi Delta, but the conditions were the same — stooped labor in extreme heat, dragging canvas sacks down rows. The valley's cotton economy was the starting point for many migrant labor circuits — families who picked valley cotton in spring would follow the harvest north through Texas, into the Midwest, and back to the valley by winter.
major_employers.narrative_hook: Valley cotton was the start of the migrant trail — if {ancestor_name} picked cotton in Hidalgo or Cameron County, the harvest season may have been just the beginning of a circuit that took the family north to West Texas, then Michigan or Minnesota, then back to the valley, following the crops in a loop that defined the year.

major_employers.name: Vegetable Farming (Onions, Cabbage, Carrots)
major_employers.wages: Piece rates and hourly rates — $0.40-0.65/hr for field work (1950s). Onion harvesting was one of the hardest jobs in the valley — pulling onions from the ground and topping them in the field, all by hand. Vegetable work filled the gaps between citrus and cotton seasons, providing year-round agricultural employment for families who stayed in the valley.
major_employers.working_conditions: Vegetable harvesting was stoop labor in its purest form — bending, pulling, cutting, and sorting in heat and humidity. Onion fields caused chronic eye irritation. The vegetable industry was less organized than citrus, with smaller growers and more reliance on labor contractors who served as intermediaries between workers and employers — and who took a percentage of the workers' earnings for the service.
major_employers.narrative_hook: Onions, cabbage, and carrots filled the months between citrus and cotton — if {ancestor_name} worked vegetables in the valley, they probably worked all three crops across the year, and the labor contractor who found them each job took a cut of every paycheck.

major_employers.name: Shrimping (Port Isabel / Brownsville)
major_employers.wages: Crew members earned shares of the catch rather than fixed wages — typically 10-20% of the haul after expenses. A good week could earn $50-100; a bad week nothing. Processing plant workers (predominantly women) earned $0.40-0.65/hr for heading and peeling shrimp.
major_employers.working_conditions: The Gulf shrimping fleet operating out of Port Isabel and Brownsville provided an alternative to field work — hard, dangerous, but sometimes more lucrative. Boats went out for days at a time. The processing plants onshore employed women and children in cold, wet conditions. Shrimping was one of the few occupations in the valley that was not controlled by Anglo grower associations, giving it a relative independence that attracted families seeking alternatives to the farm labor system.
major_employers.narrative_hook: Port Isabel's shrimping fleet was the valley's escape from the fields — if {ancestor_name} worked the Gulf boats, the work was harder than farming and the sea was unforgiving, but a shrimper answered to the weather and the catch, not to a grower with a piece rate.

housing_patterns.initial_settlement: Workers entering from Mexico typically stayed with family already established in the valley or went directly to labor camps at the grower's operation. Bracero workers were housed in designated barracks. Families settling permanently moved into the Mexican barrios or colonias — either renting a small house or buying a lot in an unincorporated subdivision on an installment plan. Housing quality ranged from adequate frame houses in established barrios to shacks without plumbing in the newer colonias.
housing_patterns.ethnic_clustering: The valley was overwhelmingly Mexican American — in most towns, Mexican families were 70-80% of the population. The clustering was not by choice but by exclusion — Anglo neighborhoods, schools, and businesses were segregated. Mexican American families lived in south-side barrios in every town or in colonias outside town limits. Within the Mexican community, families clustered by region of origin in Mexico — families from the same Mexican state or town tended to settle near each other.
housing_patterns.trajectory: Labor camp or family member's house (initial) to barrio rental (1-3 years) to colonia lot purchase on installment (5-10 years) to frame house on owned lot (10-20 years). Many families remained in the migrant labor circuit for years before settling permanently. The colonia lot — purchased on a contract-for-deed that could be revoked for a missed payment — was both a step toward stability and a trap, since the lots often lacked basic utilities.

anti_immigrant_reception.description: The Rio Grande Valley was built on the systematic dispossession and exploitation of Mexican and Mexican American people. Anglo settlers arrived after 1900 and acquired Mexican-grant lands through legal manipulation, tax sales, and outright fraud. The Texas Rangers served as an armed enforcement mechanism for Anglo land and labor interests. Segregation was comprehensive — separate schools, restaurants, swimming pools, and cemeteries. The valley's Mexican majority was politically disenfranchised through poll taxes, literacy tests, and intimidation.
specific_incidents: La Matanza / The Bandit Wars (1915-1919) — Texas Rangers and vigilantes killed hundreds (some estimates reach thousands) of Mexican and Mexican American residents in the valley during a campaign of terror following the Plan de San Diego
1930s Repatriation — valley authorities deported thousands of Mexican families, including US-born citizens, during the Depression
Operation Wetback (1954) — massive INS sweep in the valley deported over 80,000 people in the first months, with many US citizens caught in the dragnet
Segregated schools persisted into the 1960s — Mexican American children were punished for speaking Spanish
nativist_organizations: Texas Rangers (functioned as armed enforcers of Anglo land and labor interests)
Anglo growers' associations controlling labor supply and wages
Good Neighbor Commission of Texas (nominally pro-Mexican, but served to manage rather than eliminate discrimination)

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Catholic Parishes (Valley-Wide Network)
churches_and_mutual_aid.community_served: Mexican and Mexican American families — the overwhelming majority of the valley population
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Baptism, marriage, and burial records across dozens of valley parishes. The Catholic Church was the primary record-keeping institution for Mexican American families in the valley — more consistent than civil records, which often undercounted or ignored the Mexican population. Diocese of Brownsville archives contain records from across the valley.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: In the valley, the parish was the paper trail — if {ancestor_name}'s family was Catholic (and nearly everyone was), the baptism and marriage records at the local parish may be the most complete documentation of the family, because the county courthouse often did not bother to count the people who harvested its crops.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: League of United Latin American Citizens (LULAC)
churches_and_mutual_aid.community_served: Mexican American community — focused on civil rights and education
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Chapter membership records, correspondence, case files. LULAC was founded in Corpus Christi in 1929 with significant valley involvement. Valley chapters fought school segregation, poll tax discrimination, and workplace exploitation. Membership records document civic-minded families in the community.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: LULAC was born from the valley's refusal to accept second-class citizenship — if {ancestor_name} or their children were LULAC members, the organization's records document a family that fought back against the system that told Mexican Americans their labor was welcome but their presence was not.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Comisiones Honorificas Mexicanas / Mexican Consulate
churches_and_mutual_aid.community_served: Mexican nationals and Mexican American community
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Consular registration records, community event records, complaints filed against employers or authorities. The Mexican government maintained consulates in Brownsville and McAllen. Comisiones Honorificas organized patriotic celebrations and mutual aid. Consular records can document a worker's identity and employment history.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: The Mexican consulate was the one office that treated bracero workers as citizens of somewhere — if {ancestor_name} registered at the Brownsville or McAllen consulate, the records may preserve biographical details that the growers and the county courthouse never bothered to record.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: American GI Forum (Valley Chapters)
churches_and_mutual_aid.community_served: Mexican American veterans and their families
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Chapter membership records, correspondence, case files. Founded by Dr. Hector P. Garcia in Corpus Christi after a funeral home in Three Rivers TX refused to bury a Mexican American veteran. Valley chapters organized against discrimination and advocated for veterans' benefits.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: The American GI Forum was born because a Texas funeral home refused to bury a Mexican American soldier — if {ancestor_name} served in the military and came home to the valley, the GI Forum was the organization that said the country that sent you to war has no right to deny you a grave.

source_references.author: David Montejano
source_references.title: Anglos and Mexicans in the Making of Texas, 1836-1986
source_references.notes: The definitive study of Anglo-Mexican relations in South Texas — covers land dispossession, labor exploitation, the Texas Rangers, and the political economy of the valley. Essential for any narrative involving Mexican American families in South Texas.

source_references.author: Guadalupe San Miguel Jr.
source_references.title: Let All of Them Take Heed: Mexican Americans and the Campaign for Educational Equality in Texas, 1910-1981
source_references.notes: Documents the long fight against school segregation in Texas, with significant coverage of valley school districts where Mexican American children were systematically segregated and denied equal education.

source_references.author: Americo Paredes
source_references.title: With His Pistol in His Hand: A Border Ballad and Its Hero
source_references.notes: Classic study of the border corrido tradition centered on Gregorio Cortez, with deep analysis of Mexican American culture, Texas Ranger violence, and the border community's resistance narratives. Foundational text for understanding valley Mexican American identity.

source_references.author: Monica Perales
source_references.title: Smeltertown: Making and Remembering a Southwest Border Community
source_references.notes: While focused on El Paso, Perales' methodology for recovering border community history — oral history, parish records, consular records — is directly applicable to valley family research.
